text.title: Hybrid Boundary Definition and Delivery Patterns
text.summary: Five hybrid patterns, the explicit boundary rule, the tool-count constraint, and the decision framework for choosing predictive vs agile per component.

rule.statement: Assign each deliverable explicitly to one mode: high uncertainty + frequent change = agile; clear requirements + fixed scope = predictive; mixed characteristics = hybrid; continuous deployment needed = agile + DevOps. Document the assignment in program.yaml.
rule.rationale: Mode selection by team preference or political convenience — rather than risk profile — is the primary cause of hybrid program failure.

section.title: Five Hybrid Patterns
section.p: Water-Scrum-Fall: waterfall for requirements and design, Scrum for development and testing — use for hardware + software programs. Agile with Gates: agile execution inside stage-gate checkpoints — use for regulated industries (FDA, SOX). Phased Agile: multiple teams with milestone-gated phases — use for large enterprise rollouts. Selective Agile: some components agile, others predictive by risk profile. DevOps + Agile: continuous delivery with governance reviews — 2026 default for cloud-native programs.

rule.statement: Name in writing which deliverables are predictive (charter, contracts, regulatory submissions, hardware BOMs) and which are agile (software increments, user research, internal services). If you cannot draw a literal line on an architecture or org diagram, the model is incoherent — do not proceed.
rule.rationale: Boundary ambiguity is the dominant hybrid failure mode. Without an explicit line, every gate review becomes a negotiation about which rules apply.

rule.statement: Maintain one canonical work-graph (program.yaml) with milestones, epics, and team mappings. Two competing portfolios — one for governance, one for delivery — creates divergent truth that agents and humans both get wrong.
rule.rationale: Tool sprawl (backlog in Jira, gantt in MS Project, deliverables in SharePoint) means agents spend more tokens reconciling than executing.

rule.statement: Choose a tool count proportional to boundary count: ideally one tool spans both arms. Two tools maximum with an explicit sync mechanism. Three or more tools is a smell indicating an unresolved process problem.
rule.rationale: Each additional tool adds a reconciliation step; three+ tools guarantee drift between arms that becomes visible only at gate reviews.
